topic: Define, build, and modify container images
scenario: You need to deploy a custom application 'gateway' in namespace 'workloads' using a container image 'python:3.11-slim' that requires a specific command override.
tasks:
1. Create a Pod 'gateway-custom' in namespace 'workloads' using image 'python:3.11-slim'
2. Override the default command to run 'sleep 3600'
3. Set the working directory to '/tmp'
4. Add two environment variables: APP_NAME=gateway and NAMESPACE=workloads
5. Verify the container is running with the overridden command
verify: kubectl get pod gateway-custom -n workloads && kubectl describe pod gateway-custom -n workloads
hints: Use 'command' and 'args' in the container spec. WorkingDir sets the working directory.

topic: Define, build, and modify container images
scenario: Deploy 'scheduler' in 'team-alpha' with a container that needs to pull a private image 'alpine:3.18' using imagePullSecrets.
tasks:
1. Create a Secret of type 'kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson' named 'registry-creds' in 'team-alpha'
2. Create a Pod 'scheduler-private' using image 'alpine:3.18'
3. Configure imagePullSecrets to use 'registry-creds'
4. Verify the Pod can pull the image (check events if it fails)
verify: kubectl get events -n team-alpha | grep scheduler-private
hints: kubectl create secret docker-registry ... or manually create the Secret with .dockerconfigjson data.

topic: Choose and use the right workload resource (Deployment, DaemonSet, CronJob, etc.)
scenario: You need to run a background job that processes logs every 5 minutes in namespace 'default'.
tasks:
1. Create a CronJob 'backend-cron' in namespace 'default'
2. Schedule: '*/5 * * * *'
3. Job template: use image 'busybox:1.36', command: ['sh', '-c', 'echo Processing logs at $(date)']
4. Set successfulJobsHistoryLimit to 3 and failedJobsHistoryLimit to 1
5. Manually trigger one job and verify it completes
verify: kubectl get cronjob backend-cron -n default && kubectl get jobs -n default
hints: CronJob spec includes schedule, jobTemplate, and history limits. Use 'kubectl create job --from=cronjob/...' to trigger.

topic: Choose and use the right workload resource (Deployment, DaemonSet, CronJob, etc.)
scenario: You need to run a monitoring agent on every node in the cluster that collects node-level metrics.
tasks:
1. Create a DaemonSet 'cache-agent' in namespace 'dev'
2. Use image 'busybox:1.36'
3. Mount the host's /proc directory at /host/proc in read-only mode
4. Mount the host's /sys directory at /host/sys in read-only mode
5. Ensure the agent tolerates the node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane taint
6. Verify a Pod runs on every node
verify: kubectl get daemonset cache-agent -n dev && kubectl get pods -n dev -o wide
hints: DaemonSet ensures one Pod per node. Use hostPath volumes. Add tolerations for control-plane nodes.

topic: Choose and use the right workload resource (Deployment, DaemonSet, CronJob, etc.)
scenario: You need to run a one-time database migration job before starting the main application.
tasks:
1. Create a Job 'proxy-migration' in namespace 'ckad-practice'
2. Use image 'busybox:1.36' with command ['sh', '-c', 'echo DB migration completed']
3. Set completions: 1, parallelism: 1, backoffLimit: 3
4. Ensure the Job has a ttlSecondsAfterFinished of 60
5. Wait for completion and verify the Job succeeded
verify: kubectl get job proxy-migration -n ckad-practice && kubectl logs job/proxy-migration -n ckad-practice
hints: Job spec includes completions, parallelism, and backoffLimit. ttlSecondsAfterFinished auto-cleans completed Jobs.

topic: Understand multi-container Pod design patterns (sidecar, init, and others)
scenario: Create a Pod with an adapter pattern: a main app writes logs to a file, and a sidecar streams them to stdout.
tasks:
1. Create Pod 'notifier-adapter' in namespace 'workloads'
2. Main container: image 'busybox:1.36', writes logs to /var/log/app.log every 5 seconds
3. Sidecar container: image 'busybox:1.36', runs 'tail -f /var/log/app.log'
4. Share a volume 'log-vol' (emptyDir) mounted at /var/log in both containers
5. Verify sidecar stdout shows the log entries
verify: kubectl logs notifier-adapter -n workloads -c notifier-sidecar
hints: Adapter pattern transforms output. Both containers mount the same emptyDir volume.

topic: Understand multi-container Pod design patterns (sidecar, init, and others)
scenario: Deploy 'api' with an init container that waits for a database service to be ready before the main app starts.
tasks:
1. Create Pod 'api-init' in namespace 'default'
2. Init container: image 'busybox:1.36', command retries connecting to 'db-service.default.svc.cluster.local:3306' for 60 seconds
3. Main container: image 'httpd:alpine', port 3000
4. Verify init container completes before main container starts
5. Check Pod status transitions: Init:0/1 -> Init:1/1 -> Running
verify: kubectl describe pod api-init -n default | grep -A5 Init
hints: Init containers run sequentially before app containers. Use 'for i in $(seq 1 60); do ...; sleep 1; done' for retry logic.

topic: Understand multi-container Pod design patterns (sidecar, init, and others)
scenario: Create an ambassador pattern Pod where a local proxy container forwards requests to an external service.
tasks:
1. Create Pod 'cache-ambassador' in namespace 'default'
2. Ambassador container: image 'nginx:alpine', listens on port 8080
3. Configure nginx to proxy_pass to an upstream (use a simple server block)
4. Main app container: image 'busybox:1.36', runs 'wget -qO- http://localhost:8080' in a loop
5. Verify the app can reach the external service through the ambassador
verify: kubectl logs cache-ambassador -n default -c cache-app
hints: Ambassador pattern proxies external connections. The main app talks to localhost, ambassador forwards outward.

topic: Utilize persistent and ephemeral volumes
scenario: Deploy a stateful application 'processor' that requires persistent storage for its data directory.
tasks:
1. Create a PVC 'processor-pvc' in namespace 'team-alpha' requesting 1Gi storage with StorageClass 'local-path'
2. Create a Deployment 'processor-db' with 1 replica using image 'redis:7-alpine'
3. Mount the PVC at /data in the container
4. Write a test key to Redis
5. Delete the Pod and verify the data persists when the new Pod starts
verify: kubectl get pvc processor-pvc -n team-alpha && kubectl exec deployment/processor-db -n team-alpha -- redis-cli GET testkey
hints: Use volumeClaimTemplates for StatefulSets, but for Deployment use a pre-created PVC. StatefulSet is preferred for stateful apps.

topic: Utilize persistent and ephemeral volumes
scenario: Create a Pod that uses both ephemeral (emptyDir) and persistent storage.
tasks:
1. Create Pod 'notifier-mixed' in namespace 'team-beta' using image 'nginx:alpine'
2. Add an emptyDir volume 'cache' mounted at /var/cache/nginx
3. Add a hostPath volume 'logs' mounted at /var/log/nginx (host path: /tmp/nginx-logs)
4. Verify both mounts exist inside the container
5. Write a file to each mount and verify from host or container
verify: kubectl exec notifier-mixed -n team-beta -- df -h | grep -E 'cache|logs'
hints: emptyDir is ephemeral and shared between containers. hostPath mounts a directory from the node filesystem.

topic: Utilize persistent and ephemeral volumes
scenario: Create a StatefulSet with persistent volume claims for each replica.
tasks:
1. Create a headless Service 'cache' in namespace 'workloads' (clusterIP: None, ports: 8080)
2. Create a StatefulSet 'cache-stateful' with 3 replicas using image 'nginx:alpine'
3. Add a volumeClaimTemplate requesting 500Mi from StorageClass 'standard'
4. Mount the volume at /usr/share/nginx/html
5. Write unique index.html content in each Pod
6. Verify each Pod has its own persistent storage
verify: kubectl get pvc -n workloads | grep cache-stateful && kubectl exec cache-stateful-0 -n workloads -- cat /usr/share/nginx/html/index.html
hints: StatefulSet + volumeClaimTemplate creates a PVC per replica. Headless service provides stable network identity.

topic: Use Kubernetes primitives to implement common deployment strategies (blue/green or canary)
scenario: Implement a blue/green deployment for 'notifier' in namespace 'ckad-practice'.
tasks:
1. Create Deployment 'notifier-blue' with 3 replicas, image 'busybox:1.36', labels: version=blue
2. Create Service 'notifier' selector: version=blue, port 8080
3. Create Deployment 'notifier-green' with 3 replicas, image 'busybox:latest', labels: version=green
4. Verify green is ready, then switch Service selector to version=green
5. Verify traffic now routes to green Pods
verify: kubectl get endpoints notifier -n ckad-practice && kubectl describe svc notifier -n ckad-practice | grep Selector
hints: Blue/green: two parallel deployments, one Service. Switch selector to cut over instantly.

topic: Use Kubernetes primitives to implement common deployment strategies (blue/green or canary)
scenario: Implement a canary deployment for 'api' in namespace 'team-alpha'.
tasks:
1. Create Deployment 'api-stable' with 9 replicas, image 'alpine:3.18', labels: track=stable
2. Create Service 'api' selector: app=api, port 3306
3. Create Deployment 'api-canary' with 1 replica, image 'alpine:latest', labels: track=canary
4. Verify both deployments are exposed through the same Service
5. Scale canary to 5 and stable to 5 to complete the rollout
6. Delete stable deployment when canary is verified
verify: kubectl get pods -n team-alpha -l app=api --show-labels && kubectl get endpoints api -n team-alpha
hints: Canary: both deployments share the same app label but have different track labels. Service selects only app label.

topic: Understand Deployments and how to perform rolling updates
scenario: Perform a controlled rolling update of 'scheduler' in namespace 'workloads'.
tasks:
1. Create Deployment 'scheduler' with 4 replicas, image 'redis:7-alpine', port 9000
2. Set strategy: RollingUpdate, maxSurge=2, maxUnavailable=1
3. Update the image to 'redis:latest'
4. Monitor rollout status with 'kubectl rollout status'
5. Verify all Pods use the new image
6. Rollback to previous revision and confirm old image is restored
verify: kubectl rollout history deployment/scheduler -n workloads && kubectl get pods -n workloads -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].image}'
hints: maxSurge controls how many extra Pods can be created. maxUnavailable controls how many Pods can be down during update.

topic: Use the Helm package manager to deploy existing packages
scenario: Deploy a chart from a Helm repository into namespace 'dev'.
tasks:
1. Add the bitnami Helm repository: helm repo add bitnami https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami
2. Update repo index: helm repo update
3. Search for a 'nginx' chart in the bitnami repo
4. Install the chart as 'backend-nginx' in namespace 'dev' with --create-namespace
5. Verify the release with 'helm list -n dev'
6. Upgrade the release to set replicaCount=3
7. Rollback the release to revision 1
verify: helm list -n dev && helm history backend-nginx -n dev
hints: Helm manages releases with revisions. Use 'helm install', 'helm upgrade', 'helm rollback', 'helm uninstall'.

topic: Use the Helm package manager to deploy existing packages
scenario: Create a simple Helm chart for 'processor' and deploy it.
tasks:
1. Create a new Helm chart: helm create processor-chart
2. Modify values.yaml to use image 'nginx:alpine' and replicaCount=2
3. Update templates/deployment.yaml to add a readiness probe on port 80
4. Package the chart: helm package processor-chart
5. Install the chart in namespace 'team-alpha'
6. Verify the deployed application
verify: helm list -n team-alpha && kubectl get deployment -n team-alpha | grep processor
hints: Helm charts use Go templates. values.yaml feeds variables into templates.

topic: Kustomize
scenario: Use Kustomize to manage environment-specific configurations for 'webapp'.
tasks:
1. Create a base directory with a Deployment manifest for 'webapp' (image: nginx:alpine, 2 replicas)
2. Create an overlay for 'staging' that changes replicas to 3 and adds resource limits
3. Create an overlay for 'prod' that changes replicas to 5 and adds a nodeSelector
4. Build the staging overlay: kustomize build overlays/staging
5. Apply the prod overlay: kubectl apply -k overlays/prod
6. Verify the differences between environments
verify: kustomize build overlays/prod | kubectl diff -f - && kubectl get deployment webapp -n prod
hints: Kustomize uses bases + overlays. kustomization.yaml defines resources, patches, and common customizations.

topic: Understand API deprecations
scenario: Identify and migrate deprecated API usage for 'backend' in namespace 'staging'.
tasks:
1. Create a Pod manifest using an older API version that is deprecated (e.g., apps/v1beta1 if available, or simulate by checking current versions)
2. Use 'kubectl api-resources' to list available API versions
3. Check if any deprecated APIs are in use with 'kubectl get --raw /apis'
4. Update the manifest to use the current stable API version
5. Apply the updated manifest and verify
verify: kubectl api-versions | grep apps && kubectl get pod backend -n staging
hints: Check 'kubectl api-resources' and 'kubectl explain' for current API versions. The CKAD exam tests v1.35.

topic: Implement probes and health checks
scenario: Create a robust health checking setup for 'frontend' in namespace 'dev'.
tasks:
1. Create Deployment 'frontend' with 2 replicas, image 'nginx:alpine', port 5000
2. Add livenessProbe: HTTP GET /healthz, initialDelaySeconds=10, periodSeconds=5
3. Add readinessProbe: HTTP GET /ready, initialDelaySeconds=5, periodSeconds=3
4. Add startupProbe: HTTP GET /startup, failureThreshold=18, periodSeconds=5
5. Verify probes are configured with 'kubectl describe pod'
6. Intentionally return 500 from /healthz and observe restart behavior
verify: kubectl get pod -n dev -l app=frontend && kubectl describe pod -n dev -l app=frontend | grep -A5 Events
hints: Startup probe disables liveness and readiness until it succeeds. This prevents premature restarts of slow-starting apps.

topic: Implement probes and health checks
scenario: Configure TCP socket and exec probes for 'notifier' in namespace 'team-alpha'.
tasks:
1. Create Pod 'notifier-tcp' in namespace 'team-alpha' running 'busybox:1.36' on port 9000
2. Add livenessProbe: TCP socket check on port 9000, initialDelaySeconds=15
3. Add readinessProbe: exec command ['test', '-f', '/tmp/ready']
4. Exec into the Pod and create /tmp/ready to mark it ready
5. Remove /tmp/ready and observe the Pod becoming not-ready
6. Restore /tmp/ready and verify it becomes ready again
verify: kubectl get pod notifier-tcp -n team-alpha && kubectl exec notifier-tcp -n team-alpha -- touch /tmp/ready
hints: TCP probes check port connectivity. Exec probes run commands inside the container. Use 'kubectl exec' to manipulate readiness.

topic: Use built-in CLI tools to monitor Kubernetes applications
scenario: Monitor resource usage and status of 'processor' in namespace 'team-beta'.
tasks:
1. Create Deployment 'processor' with 3 replicas and resource requests
2. Use 'kubectl top pod -n team-beta' to check CPU/memory usage (requires metrics-server)
3. Use 'kubectl top node' to check node-level resources
4. Watch Pod status changes with 'kubectl get pods -n team-beta -w'
5. Sort Pods by restart count: 'kubectl get pods -n team-beta --sort-by='.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount''
6. Use 'kubectl get events -n team-beta --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | tail -20'
verify: kubectl top pod -n team-beta 2>/dev/null || echo 'metrics-server not available - simulate with kubectl get pods'
hints: metrics-server provides top metrics. If not installed, focus on kubectl get/describe/events commands.

topic: Utilize container logs
scenario: Collect and analyze logs from 'processor' in namespace 'prod'.
tasks:
1. Create Deployment 'processor' that outputs structured logs to stdout
2. View logs from a single Pod: 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n prod'
3. View logs from all Pods in the Deployment: 'kubectl logs -l app=processor -n prod'
4. Stream logs in real-time: 'kubectl logs -f deployment/processor -n prod'
5. View previous container logs (after a restart): 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n prod --previous'
6. Save logs to a file for analysis
verify: kubectl logs -l app=processor -n prod --tail=10
hints: Use labels to aggregate logs across replicas. --previous shows logs from crashed containers.

topic: Debugging in Kubernetes
scenario: A Deployment 'api' in namespace 'ckad-practice' is failing. Debug and fix it.
tasks:
1. Investigate why Pods are in CrashLoopBackOff using 'kubectl describe pod' and 'kubectl logs'
2. Check for common issues: wrong image name, missing ConfigMap/Secret, resource limits too low
3. Verify events with 'kubectl get events -n ckad-practice | grep api'
4. Fix the identified issue (e.g., correct the image tag or add missing env var)
5. Verify the Deployment recovers and Pods reach Running state
verify: kubectl get deployment api -n ckad-practice && kubectl get pods -n ckad-practice -l app=api
hints: Debugging flow: describe pod -> check events -> check logs -> fix manifest -> re-apply.

topic: Debugging in Kubernetes
scenario: A Service 'frontend' in namespace 'team-alpha' is not routing traffic correctly.
tasks:
1. Verify Service exists and has endpoints: 'kubectl get svc frontend -n team-alpha' and 'kubectl get endpoints frontend -n team-alpha'
2. Check if selector labels match Pod labels
3. Verify Pods are ready (readiness probe passing)
4. Test connectivity from a debug Pod: 'kubectl run debug --rm -it --image=busybox -n team-alpha -- wget -qO- http://frontend'
5. Check for NetworkPolicy blocking traffic
6. Fix any mismatched labels or policies and verify
verify: kubectl get endpoints frontend -n team-alpha && kubectl get pods -n team-alpha -l app=frontend -o wide
hints: No endpoints = selector mismatch or no ready Pods. Use a debug Pod to test network paths.

topic: Discover and use resources that extend Kubernetes (CRD, Operators)
scenario: Work with a Custom Resource Definition for 'api' in namespace 'prod'.
tasks:
1. List existing CRDs: 'kubectl get crds'
2. Inspect a specific CRD: 'kubectl get crd <crd-name> -o yaml'
3. Create a custom resource instance of an existing CRD (if available) or describe how you would
4. View the CRD's defined schema and validation
5. Explain the relationship between CRDs and Operators
verify: kubectl get crds && kubectl get crd <crd-name> -o jsonpath='{.spec.names}'
hints: CRDs extend the Kubernetes API. Operators use CRDs to manage complex applications.

topic: Understand authentication, authorization, and admission control
scenario: Set up RBAC for 'proxy' in namespace 'ckad-practice' with fine-grained permissions.
tasks:
1. Create a Role 'proxy-role' in namespace 'ckad-practice' allowing get/list/watch on Pods and Services only
2. Create a RoleBinding 'proxy-binding' associating the Role with user 'developer'
3. Verify permissions with 'kubectl auth can-i get pods --as=developer -n ckad-practice'
4. Verify 'kubectl auth can-i delete pods --as=developer -n ckad-practice' returns 'no'
5. Create a ClusterRole for reading nodes, bind it to group 'devops'
6. Explain the difference between Role (namespace-scoped) and ClusterRole (cluster-scoped)
verify: kubectl auth can-i get pods --as=developer -n ckad-practice && kubectl auth can-i delete pods --as=developer -n ckad-practice
hints: Role is namespace-scoped. ClusterRole is cluster-wide. Use RoleBinding for Role, ClusterRoleBinding for ClusterRole.

topic: Understand requests, limits, quotas
scenario: Enforce resource constraints for namespace 'team-alpha'.
tasks:
1. Create a ResourceQuota 'cache-quota' in namespace 'team-alpha' with:
   - requests.cpu=2, requests.memory=4Gi
   - limits.cpu=4, limits.memory=8Gi
   - pods=10, services=5
2. Create a LimitRange 'cache-limits' in namespace 'team-alpha' with:
   - default CPU limit=500m, default memory limit=512Mi
   - default request CPU=100m, default request memory=128Mi
3. Create a Deployment that exceeds the quota and observe the failure
4. Adjust the Deployment to fit within quota and verify it runs
verify: kubectl get resourcequota cache-quota -n team-alpha && kubectl describe resourcequota cache-quota -n team-alpha
hints: ResourceQuota limits total resources in a namespace. LimitRange sets defaults for containers.

topic: Understand ConfigMaps
scenario: Configure 'webapp' in namespace 'prod' using ConfigMaps in multiple ways.
tasks:
1. Create ConfigMap 'webapp-config' from literal values: APP_NAME=webapp, LOG_LEVEL=debug, PORT=6379
2. Create a second ConfigMap 'webapp-files' from a file (create a local file 'settings.conf' with key=value data first)
3. Create a Pod that consumes 'webapp-config' as environment variables
4. Mount 'webapp-files' as a volume at /etc/config in the same Pod
5. Verify env vars and mounted files from inside the container
6. Update the ConfigMap and observe if changes reflect in the Pod (env vars don't update, mounted files do)
verify: kubectl exec webapp-pod -n prod -- env | grep APP_NAME && kubectl exec webapp-pod -n prod -- ls /etc/config
hints: ConfigMap as env: updates require Pod restart. ConfigMap as volume: updates reflect automatically (after kubelet sync period).

topic: Define resource requirements
scenario: Properly size 'notifier' in namespace 'team-beta' with CPU and memory constraints.
tasks:
1. Create Deployment 'notifier' with image 'nginx:alpine', 2 replicas
2. Set resource requests: CPU=200m, memory=1Gi
3. Set resource limits: CPU=500m, memory=512Mi
4. Verify resources are set with 'kubectl describe pod'
5. Check if the Pod is assigned to a node with sufficient resources
6. Explain the difference between requests (scheduling) and limits (runtime enforcement)
verify: kubectl get pod -n team-beta -l app=notifier -o jsonpath='{.items[0].spec.containers[0].resources}'
hints: Requests are used by scheduler. Limits are enforced by kubelet/Cgroups. Always set both.

topic: Create & consume Secrets
scenario: Securely manage sensitive data for 'cache' in namespace 'team-alpha'.
tasks:
1. Create Secret 'cache-secrets' in namespace 'team-alpha' with keys: TLS_CERT, API_KEY, AUTH_TOKEN (use generic type with --from-literal)
2. Create a Pod that consumes the Secret as environment variables
3. Create a second Pod that mounts the Secret as files in /etc/secrets
4. Verify env vars don't show values in 'kubectl describe pod' (only reference the Secret)
5. Verify files in /etc/secrets contain the decoded values
6. Create a TLS Secret from certificate files (simulate with self-signed cert if needed)
verify: kubectl get secret cache-secrets -n team-alpha -o jsonpath='{.data}' && kubectl exec cache-pod -n team-alpha -- cat /etc/secrets/TLS_CERT
hints: Secrets are base64-encoded in etcd. Mounted as files = decoded automatically. As env vars = available decoded in container.

topic: Understand ServiceAccounts
scenario: Configure a dedicated ServiceAccount for 'notifier' in namespace 'team-alpha'.
tasks:
1. Create ServiceAccount 'notifier-sa' in namespace 'team-alpha'
2. Create a Role allowing get/list on ConfigMaps in 'team-alpha'
3. Create a RoleBinding binding 'notifier-sa' to the Role
4. Create a Pod that uses 'notifier-sa' as its serviceAccountName
5. From inside the Pod, attempt to list ConfigMaps (should succeed) and Pods (should fail)
6. View the auto-mounted token at /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token
verify: kubectl auth can-i get configmaps --as=system:serviceaccount:team-alpha:notifier-sa -n team-alpha && kubectl auth can-i get pods --as=system:serviceaccount:team-alpha:notifier-sa -n team-alpha
hints: Default ServiceAccount has minimal permissions. Custom ServiceAccounts need explicit RoleBindings.

topic: Understand Application Security (SecurityContexts, Capabilities, etc.)
scenario: Harden 'cache' in namespace 'ckad-practice' with comprehensive security settings.
tasks:
1. Create Pod 'cache-secure' in namespace 'ckad-practice' with image 'python:3.11-slim'
2. Set pod-level SecurityContext: runAsNonRoot=true, seccompProfile type=RuntimeDefault
3. Set container-level SecurityContext: readOnlyRootFilesystem=true, allowPrivilegeEscalation=false, capabilities drop=['ALL']
4. Add capabilities add=['NET_BIND_SERVICE'] if the app needs to bind to privileged ports
5. Verify the container runs as a non-root user
6. Verify the root filesystem is read-only and the container cannot write to /
verify: kubectl exec cache-secure -n ckad-practice -- id && kubectl exec cache-secure -n ckad-practice -- touch /testfile 2>&1 | grep 'Read-only file system'
hints: SecurityContext at pod level affects all containers. Container level overrides pod level. Drop ALL capabilities, then add only what's needed.

topic: Understand Application Security (SecurityContexts, Capabilities, etc.)
scenario: Run 'processor' as a specific user with restricted permissions in namespace 'team-beta'.
tasks:
1. Create Pod 'processor-restricted' in namespace 'team-beta' with image 'nginx:alpine'
2. Set runAsUser=1000, runAsGroup=1000, fsGroup=1000 at pod level
3. Verify the nginx process runs as UID 1000 inside the container
4. Mount an emptyDir volume and verify files are created with group ownership 1000
5. Add runAsNonRoot=true and verify the Pod fails if the image tries to run as root
verify: kubectl exec processor-restricted -n team-beta -- ps aux && kubectl exec processor-restricted -n team-beta -- ls -la /usr/share/nginx/html
hints: fsGroup sets ownership for volumes. runAsUser overrides the image's default user.

topic: Demonstrate basic understanding of NetworkPolicies
scenario: Restrict network traffic for 'processor' in namespace 'default'.
tasks:
1. Create a default-deny NetworkPolicy in namespace 'default' that blocks all ingress and egress
2. Create an allow NetworkPolicy 'processor-allow' that allows ingress to Pods with label app=processor only from Pods with label app=frontend in the same namespace
3. Allow egress from 'processor' Pods to port 3000 on CIDR 10.0.0.0/8 only
4. Test connectivity from allowed and denied sources using debug Pods
5. Verify allowed traffic succeeds and denied traffic times out
verify: kubectl get networkpolicy -n default && kubectl describe networkpolicy processor-allow -n default
hints: Default deny: empty ingress/egress rules with policyTypes. Always specify both policyTypes if you want to control both directions.

topic: Demonstrate basic understanding of NetworkPolicies
scenario: Allow cross-namespace communication for 'processor' between 'default' and 'ckad-practice'.
tasks:
1. In namespace 'default', create a Pod with label app=processor-backend
2. In namespace 'ckad-practice', create a Pod with label app=processor-frontend
3. Create a NetworkPolicy in 'default' allowing ingress from namespace 'ckad-practice' to Pods labeled app=processor-backend
4. Test connectivity from the frontend Pod to the backend Pod
5. Verify that Pods from other namespaces cannot reach the backend
verify: kubectl get networkpolicy -n default && kubectl exec -n ckad-practice processor-frontend -- wget -qO- http://processor-backend.default.svc.cluster.local
hints: Use namespaceSelector in NetworkPolicy to allow traffic from specific namespaces.

topic: Provide and troubleshoot access to applications via services
scenario: Expose 'webapp' in namespace 'team-beta' using multiple Service types.
tasks:
1. Create Deployment 'webapp' with 3 replicas and label app=webapp
2. Create ClusterIP Service 'webapp-internal' on port 5000, targetPort 80
3. Create NodePort Service 'webapp-nodeport' on port 5000, nodePort 30080
4. Verify endpoints are populated for both Services
5. Test internal access from a Pod in the same namespace
6. Explain when to use ClusterIP vs NodePort vs LoadBalancer
verify: kubectl get svc -n team-beta | grep webapp && kubectl get endpoints -n team-beta | grep webapp
hints: ClusterIP = internal only. NodePort = exposes on each node's IP. LoadBalancer = cloud provider external IP.

topic: Provide and troubleshoot access to applications via services
scenario: Troubleshoot service discovery between 'processor' and 'cache' in namespace 'team-alpha'.
tasks:
1. Create Deployment and Service for 'processor' with selector app=processor
2. Create Deployment and Service for 'cache' with selector app=cache
3. Verify DNS resolution works: 'nslookup cache.team-alpha.svc.cluster.local' from a debug Pod
4. Test connectivity with curl/wget between the two Services
5. Intentionally break the selector label on one Service and observe endpoints disappear
6. Fix the selector and verify endpoints return
verify: kubectl get endpoints processor cache -n team-alpha && kubectl run debug --rm -it -n team-alpha --image=busybox -- nslookup cache
hints: Service DNS: <service>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local. Endpoints disappear if selector doesn't match Pods.

topic: Use Ingress rules to expose applications
scenario: Expose 'gateway' and 'processor' externally using Ingress in namespace 'staging'.
tasks:
1. Deploy 'gateway' and 'processor' each with their own ClusterIP Service
2. Create an Ingress resource 'gateway-ingress' routing host 'gateway.example.com' to gateway service
3. Add a second rule in the same Ingress routing path '/processor' to processor service
4. Verify the Ingress has an address assigned (if ingress controller is running)
5. Test with curl using the host header: 'curl -H "Host: gateway.example.com" http://<ingress-ip>/'
6. Add TLS configuration with a self-signed certificate (optional advanced)
verify: kubectl get ingress -n staging && kubectl describe ingress gateway-ingress -n staging
hints: Ingress needs an Ingress controller (e.g., NGINX Ingress). For kind, install with 'kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/ingress-nginx/main/deploy/static/provider/kind/deploy.yaml'.
